text:
In contrast to the customized synthetic strategies typically used to construct specific natural products, biosynthetic pathways often leverage the divergent cyclization of a common polyunsaturated intermediate to generate structural variety. Taking inspiration from these natural processes, we report a flexible, divergent synthetic platform for the efficient assembly of monoterpene indole alkaloids. Central to this approach is the development of a pluripotent dihydropyridine-vinylindole intermediate. By modulating the reactivity of this common precursor through specific functional group manipulations and reaction conditions, we achieved systematic access to five distinct skeletal frameworks—including the aspidosperma, iboga, andranginine, and ngouniensine types, as well as a non-natural variant—in only 6 to 9 steps from tryptamine.

Key to our methodology is a copper(I)-catalyzed 6-endo-dig cyclization that forms the sensitive dihydropyridine core under mild conditions. We demonstrate the utility of this strategy through three distinct modes of [4+2] cyclizations and two redox-mediated annulations. The practical efficiency and versatility of the assembly line were further validated by the total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine, (±)-andranginine, and (−)-catharanthine, with the latter representing the shortest enantiocontrolled synthesis reported to date. This work establishes a programmable framework for the diversification of complex architectures, offering a blueprint for emulating biogenetic divergence in the laboratory.
